theft operations often progress rapidly from access to illicit blockchain transactions within days. - Supply chain compromises: malicious npm/PyPI packages and dependencies to target developers and projects [T1195.001]. - Impersonation of developers on GitHub; inviting targets to collaborate on repositories containing malicious dependencies. - Cloud supply-chain attack on a cloud identity management provider in July 2023, pushing malicious updates to cryptocurrency industry customers [T1195.002][T1199]. - 2024 incident: developer lured into running malicious Python leading to theft of 4,502.9 BTC (~$308M) via diverted transactions. - 2024–2025 activity included use of malicious Docker images [T1609], domain-registered C2 infrastructure, and theft of AWS session tokens with attempts to register virtual MFA for persistence. - Cloud reconnaissance: enumeration of IAM roles, S3 buckets, and other assets [T1580]. - Frontend tampering: injection of malicious JavaScript into a statically hosted Next.js app to detect and modify cryptocurrency transactions in real time [T1578.005]. - Operations include diverting legitimate transactions and laundering stolen funds to support state priorities.
